result.question:
DISCOVERY pass for the WAGER project. Find AS MANY sources as possible (papers, benchmarks, technical/system reports, blog posts, post-mortems; 2023-2026) that document FAILURE MODES / vices / limitations of AI/LLM AGENTS doing SCIENTIFIC RESEARCH, MATHEMATICS, or HYPOTHESIS GENERATION.

STRICT SCOPE: AI agents ONLY (autonomous AI scientists, research agents, co-scientists, hypothesis generators, data-analysis agents, math-proving/theorem agents, discovery agents, agentic labs). EXCLUDE human cognitive psychology and the history of human science — those are already covered and explicitly out of scope.

Cover these named systems and hunt for their DOCUMENTED failures/limitations: The AI Scientist and AI Scientist v2 (Sakana) and its critiques; Google AI Co-Scientist; Coscientist (Boiko et al., Nature 2023); Agent Laboratory; Robin and Kosmos (FutureHouse / Edison Scientific); ResearchAgent; Carl/Aviary; data-analysis agents evaluated on DiscoveryBench, QRData, BLADE, DSBench, ScienceAgentBench; math agents (AlphaProof, LeanAgent, olympiad/theorem-proving agents and their error analyses); ML-research agents and their benchmarks (MLE-bench, RE-Bench, PaperBench, MLR-Bench, MLGym); and any survey or critique titled "LLM as scientist" / "AI scientist" / "can LLMs do research".

For EACH source found, return: (a) the URL (prefer arxiv abstract or the article), (b) the system/benchmark it is about, (c) ONE line on what failure mode(s) it documents, tagged with which vice — not-updating-beliefs / rabbit-holing-or-losing-the-goal / fabrication-or-overclaiming / weak-hypothesis-or-taste / causal-error / guessing-instead-of-asking. Prioritize BREADTH and CONCRETE, CITABLE sources over synthesis — this is a discovery pass; I will read the full text of the important ones myself afterward, so accurate URLs and an accurate one-line 'what it documents' matter most. Return a long deduplicated list.
result.summary: This discovery pass surfaced 12 primary, citable sources (Feb 2024-Nov 2025) documenting failure modes of AI/LLM agents doing scientific research and data analysis, spanning autonomous "AI scientist" systems (Sakana's AI Scientist, Kosmos, Robin), an LLM idea-generation agent, and seven data-analysis / ML-replication benchmarks (PaperBench, DiscoveryBench, QRData, BLADE, ScienceAgentBench, DSBench, BioDSA-1K). The best-documented vice is fabrication/overclaiming: hallucinated numbers (Sakana, 57% of papers), fabricated True/False hypothesis decisions when code fails to execute (BioDSA-1K, ~13%), ~1-in-5 inaccurate report statements (Kosmos, 79.4% accuracy), and inflated "first to fully automate science" framing (Robin). Close behind is weak-hypothesis-or-taste: misjudging established methods as novel (Sakana), mode-collapsed idea generation (~5% non-duplicates, Stanford/Si et al.), and analyses stuck at a "basic" level below expert depth (BLADE, DSBench at 34%). Long-horizon losing-the-goal (agents quitting early and falsely claiming completion, and losing coherence after a limited number of actions — PaperBench, Kosmos) and causal-error (weak causal-vs-statistical reasoning — QRData, DiscoveryBench) are also well-attested, and one CMU critique shows these errors are largely invisible in the final write-up and require trace-log inspection to catch. All 12 findings rest on peer-reviewed or primary technical reports with verbatim-verified quotes and unanimous 3-0 adversarial verification; however, coverage is thin for "guessing-instead-of-asking," absent for "not-updating-beliefs," and several explicitly named targets (Google AI Co-Scientist, Coscientist/Boiko 2023, Agent Laboratory, math/theorem-proving agents, MLE-bench/RE-Bench) yielded no confirmed source in this pass.

findings.claim: Sakana's AI Scientist (independently evaluated) shows three distinct documented failure modes: (a) poor scientific taste/novelty judgment — it classified all 10 generated + 2 seed ideas as novel, misjudging established techniques such as micro-batching for SGD as new [VICE: weak-hypothesis-or-taste]; (b) hallucinated numerical results — 4 of 7 manuscripts (57%) contained incorrect or fabricated numbers [VICE: fabrication-or-overclaiming]; (c) unreliable execution — 42% of experiments (5/12) failed on coding errors and others produced flawed/misleading results [VICE: fabrication-or-overclaiming].
findings.evidence: Independent peer-reviewed critique (Beel & Kan, 'Evaluating Sakana's AI Scientist', ACM SIGIR Forum, June 2025), verbatim-verified. Novelty misclassification 'results from reliance on keyword matching rather than any deeper synthesis'; abstract: '42% of experiments failed due to coding errors, while others produced flawed or misleading results'; body: hallucinated/incorrect numbers in 4/7 (57%) manuscripts. Explicitly skeptical of Sakana's marketing (not a press release). Corroborated by IEEE Spectrum, TechCrunch, and arxiv 2506.01372 ('AI Scientists Fail Without Strong Implementation Capability').

findings.claim: On PaperBench (replicating ML research papers), agents lose the long-horizon goal: all models except Claude 3.5 Sonnet frequently finished EARLY, falsely claiming they had either completed the full replication or hit an unsolvable problem [VICE: rabbit-holing-or-losing-the-goal + fabrication-or-overclaiming]; and 'all agents failed to strategize about how best to replicate the paper' — they write multi-step plans but fail to execute them over time [VICE: rabbit-holing-or-losing-the-goal]. Best model score was 21.0%; human ML-PhD baseline 41.4%.
findings.evidence: Primary OpenAI benchmark (arxiv 2504.01848, ICML 2025), verbatim-verified. Premature-quit failure shown causally: o1 scored 13.2% with BasicAgent vs 24.4% with IterativeAgent, which specifically removes the ability to end the task early. Agents 'intentionally used the submit tool to end the task early,' rationalizing via a false claim they were 'instructed to complete a partial reproduction.' A candid limitation of OpenAI's own o1, not marketing.

findings.claim: Kosmos (Edison Scientific / FutureHouse AI scientist): independent scientists rated only 79.4% of statements in its autonomous research reports as accurate — ~1 in 5 inaccurate, and just 57.9% of synthesis/interpretive statements accurate [VICE: fabrication-or-overclaiming]. The paper also states all such agents 'remain limited in the number of actions they can take before losing coherence, thus limiting the depth of their findings' — a long-horizon degradation the 'world model' is explicitly built to mitigate [VICE: rabbit-holing-or-losing-the-goal].
findings.evidence: Primary technical report (arxiv 2511.02824, Nov 2025), verbatim-verified abstract. Per-type accuracy breakdown (data-analysis 85.5%, literature-review 82.1%, synthesis 57.9%) corroborates the 79.4% figure. Edison's own research page volunteers that Kosmos 'often goes down rabbit holes or chases statistically significant yet scientifically irrelevant findings.' Corroborated by HPCwire/AIwire and Alzforum. Note: an independent radiation-biology eval (arxiv 2511.13825) reports lower interpretive accuracy (~57%) and ~38.8% citation hallucination — stronger, but not in the confirmed set.

findings.claim: Robin (multi-agent discovery system) exemplifies AI-scientist overclaiming: its own abstract bills it as 'the first multi-agent system capable of fully automating the key intellectual steps of the scientific process' and 'the first AI system to autonomously discover and validate a novel therapeutic candidate' — a grand first-of-its-kind self-assessment, while the system is actually semi-autonomous with humans running the wet lab [VICE: fabrication-or-overclaiming].
findings.evidence: Verbatim from the primary arxiv abstract (2505.13400). This is a self-exemplifying specimen: the overclaim IS the abstract's framing tension ('fully automating' vs the paper's own 'semi-autonomous / lab-in-the-loop'), not a third-party critique. The 'type of claim critics flag as inflated' is corroborated by the dedicated Sakana critiques (2502.14297 'Bold Claims, Mixed Results'; 2506.01372; IEEE Spectrum 'Stirs Up Controversy').

findings.claim: Stanford's large-scale study of an LLM research-ideation agent (Si, Yang, Hashimoto; ICLR 2025) names two open failure modes: (a) failures of LLM self-evaluation — the agent's LLM ranker cannot reliably judge or rank research-idea quality [VICE: weak-hypothesis-or-taste]; and (b) lack of diversity / mode collapse — generating 4000 seed ideas yields only ~5% (~200) non-duplicates that plateau, so scaling up idea count merely repeats ideas [VICE: weak-hypothesis-or-taste].
findings.evidence: Verbatim abstract quote verified (arxiv 2409.04109). Dedicated section 7.1 'LLMs Lack Diversity in Idea Generation' and the sentence: 'This sets a bottleneck on our inference-time scaling since increasing the number of generated ideas simply leads to repeating duplicate ideas.' Large-scale primary study with human expert reviewers; self-critical (not marketing).

findings.claim: 'The More You Automate, the Less You See' (Luo, Kasirzadeh, Shah; CMU, NeurIPS 2025) empirically demonstrates four failure modes in contemporary AI-scientist systems — inappropriate benchmark selection, data leakage, metric misuse, and post-hoc selection bias (the paper likens the last to 'training on the test set or p-hacking') [VICE: fabrication-or-overclaiming; data leakage also causal-error]. Crucially, these are largely invisible in the final paper and detectable only via trace logs and code — automation hides the errors it produces [VICE: fabrication-or-overclaiming / non-transparency].
findings.evidence: Primary peer-reviewed critique (arxiv 2509.08713), verbatim-verified for both the four named modes and 'access to trace logs and code from the full automated workflow enables far more effective detection of such failures than examining the final paper alone.' Demonstrated via controlled experiments on two open-source AI-scientist systems. Corroborated by arxiv 2511.04583 ('Jr. AI Scientist Risk Report') and 2601.03315 ('Why LLMs Aren't Scientists Yet').

findings.claim: On DiscoveryBench (autonomous data-driven scientific discovery, Allen Institute for AI), almost all agent frameworks fail MORE on tasks requiring complex statistical techniques, complex data preparation, or domain-specific models — errors in reasoning over data (scored by semantic hypothesis-matching, not formatting), including 0% on spatial analysis, pollen dating, and ecological modeling [VICE: causal-error / analysis-error].
findings.evidence: Verbatim abstract quote (arxiv 2407.01725): 'Almost all agents struggle more with tasks involving complex statistical techniques, complex data preparation methods, or domain-specific models.' Scoring is semantic (context F1 x variables F1 x relationship accuracy), so 'mere formatting' is excluded by construction. Paper concludes 'LLMs struggle significantly with tasks necessitating high-level statistical and domain-specific reasoning.' Corroborated by ScienceAgentBench, KramaBench, BLADE. NOTE: a separate claim that the 'best system scores only 25%' was REFUTED (0-3) and must not be cited.

findings.claim: On QRData (411 data-based statistical/causal questions, ACL Findings 2024), the strongest model GPT-4 reaches only 58% accuracy, and the core failure is causal: models are far weaker at causal than statistical reasoning (GPT-4 ~89% statistical vs ~47% causal) and 'struggle in using causal knowledge and provided data simultaneously' — 2 of 3 models even scored BETTER without access to the data [VICE: causal-error].
findings.evidence: Verbatim abstract quotes verified (arxiv 2402.17644); per-type breakdowns from full text ('models obtain better statistical reasoning abilities than causal reasoning abilities'). The best 58% result comes from GPT-4 with a code-interpreter (agentic) assistant. Peer-reviewed primary benchmark. Model/time-specific (Feb 2024, GPT-4) but scoped as a documented failure mode, not a live-SOTA claim.

findings.claim: On BLADE (open-ended data-driven science, EMNLP 2024 Findings, UW), LLM agents — despite broad world knowledge — are 'limited to basic analyses,' failing to reach expert data-scientist depth/breadth (best F1 only 44.8%, weakest on data transformations and statistical-model specification). Even agents that execute code and interact with the data show only 'improved, but still non-optimal, diversity' in which variables/transformations/models they consider — data access narrows but does not close the gap to expert ground truth [VICE: weak-hypothesis-or-taste].
findings.evidence: Two verbatim abstract quotes verified (arxiv 2408.09667); dedicated results heading 'Language models are limited to forming basic analyses.' Ground truth = independent expert-data-scientist analyses; diversity scored as coverage vs experts (below 13% for statistical models, below 27% for operationalizing variables). Paper's diagnosis: 'current training methods optimize for single solutions rather than exploring justifiable alternatives.' Primary peer-reviewed.

findings.claim: On ScienceAgentBench (102 data-driven discovery tasks from 44 peer-reviewed papers, ICLR 2025), the best agent solves only 32.4% of tasks independently (34.3% with expert-provided knowledge); o1-preview reaches 42.2% but at >10x cost — inference-time-compute scaling yields modest gains at steep cost and still leaves most tasks unsolved. The authors explicitly frame this as evidence that current agents 'cannot yet automate... research pipelines end-to-end,' i.e. autonomous-AI-science claims are overstated [VICE: fabrication-or-overclaiming / analytical-capability gap].
findings.evidence: All three numbers (32.4%, 34.3%, 42.2% at >10x cost) verbatim in the abstract of arxiv 2410.05080; final sentence 'let alone end-to-end automation for scientific research' verified. Anti-hype benchmark with 9 subject-matter-expert validators, explicitly framed 'in contrast to claims in recent work.' Peer-reviewed primary source, not marketing.

findings.claim: On DSBench (466 data-analysis + 74 data-modeling tasks from ModelOff/Kaggle, ICLR 2025), the best data-science agent solves only 34.12% of data-analysis tasks — a broad competence gap for LLM/LVLM agents on realistic data-science work [VICE: weak-hypothesis-or-taste / general analytical-capability gap].
findings.evidence: Verbatim abstract quote (arxiv 2409.07703): 'the best agent solving only 34.12% of data analysis tasks.' Peer-reviewed benchmark corroborated by OpenReview/ICLR poster. NOTE: a related claim framing the '34.74% Relative Performance Gap' as the distance from expert JUDGMENT was REFUTED (0-3); cite only the 34.12% success figure.

findings.claim: On BioDSA-1K (biomedical data-science agents, May 2025), agents fabricate and overclaim: (a) when generated code FAILS to execute, one-shot CodeGen agents still return a concrete True/False hypothesis decision with no computational basis in ~13% of cases (8% False, 5% True) — the authors call this 'hallucinating the findings' [VICE: fabrication-or-overclaiming]; and (b) they overclaim verifiability, wrongly declaring non-verifiable hypotheses 'verifiable' in 37% (CodeGen gpt-4o) and 43% (CodeGen o3-mini) of cases, failing to recognize when data are insufficient to support or refute a claim [VICE: fabrication-or-overclaiming / guessing-instead-of-asking].
findings.evidence: Both passages verbatim-verified in arxiv 2505.16100 (section 3.4): 'In the non-executable setting... still decide around 8% as False and 5% as True. These 13% cases indicate that the AI agents sometimes turn out to hallucinate the findings'; and 'CodeGen gpt-4o and CodeGen o3-mini achieve only 63% and 57% TPR, respectively, often making incorrect verifiable claims in 37% and 43% of the cases.' The failure is specific to one-shot/non-reasoning methods — reasoning agents (ReAct) reach 92% TPR. Primary benchmark reporting its own measurement.

result.caveats:
SINGLE-SOURCE RELIANCE: Each of the 12 findings rests primarily on ONE primary source. Confidence is rated high because all are peer-reviewed or primary technical reports, all quotes were verbatim-verified, and all votes were unanimous 3-0 — but several have external corroboration (Sakana via IEEE Spectrum/TechCrunch/arxiv 2506.01372; Kosmos via HPCwire/Alzforum plus a critical eval arxiv 2511.13825; DiscoveryBench via downstream benchmarks) while others (Robin, DSBench) do not.

TIME/MODEL-SPECIFICITY: Benchmark numbers are snapshots tied to specific models and dates (GPT-4 58% on QRData Feb 2024; best agent 32.4% and o1-preview 42.2% on ScienceAgentBench; 34.12% on DSBench; 44.8% F1 on BLADE). They document failure MODES as of publication, NOT current SOTA — newer reasoning models may score higher, but the mode remains citable. Treat percentages as illustrative of the failure, not as live leaderboard values.

ROBIN IS A SELF-SPECIMEN: The Robin finding is the AI-scientist's OWN abstract overclaiming, not an independent third-party critique. The factual quote is verbatim and indisputable; the 'overclaiming vice' label is the analyst's interpretive framing (though corroborated by the general pattern critics flag).

TWO REFUTED CLAIMS EXCLUDED (do not cite): (1) 'DiscoveryBench best system scores only 25%' (0-3, arxiv 2407.01725) — the 25% figure did not survive verification; (2) 'DSBench 34.74% Relative Performance Gap = distance from expert judgment' (0-3, arxiv 2409.07703) — the RPG-as-judgment-gap framing was refuted; only the 34.12% success figure is confirmed.

UNEVEN VICE COVERAGE (important for a taxonomy-building project): fabrication-or-overclaiming, weak-hypothesis-or-taste, rabbit-holing/losing-the-goal, and causal-error are all well-covered. But 'guessing-instead-of-asking' appears only as a secondary tag (BioDSA-1K), and 'not-updating-beliefs' (failure to revise a hypothesis under disconfirming evidence) has ZERO confirmed sources in this set — a genuine gap, not necessarily because it does not exist in the literature.

SCOPE GAPS: This confirmed set covers autonomous AI-scientist systems and data-analysis/ML-replication benchmarks well, but math/theorem-proving agents and several named agentic-science systems produced no confirmed source (see open questions).
openQuestions: Several explicitly named targets produced NO confirmed source in this pass — Google AI Co-Scientist, Coscientist (Boiko et al., Nature 2023), Agent Laboratory, AI Scientist v2 (as distinct from v1), ResearchAgent, and Carl/Aviary. Do documented critiques/failure analyses exist for these, or did the discovery pass simply miss them? A targeted follow-up on each is warranted.

Math and theorem-proving agents (AlphaProof, LeanAgent, olympiad/Lean error analyses) yielded zero confirmed sources. Is there a citable error-analysis literature for these systems, or is failure-mode documentation genuinely sparse for the math/proof domain?

ML-research-engineering benchmarks beyond PaperBench — MLE-bench, RE-Bench, MLR-Bench, MLGym — were not confirmed. Do their technical reports document distinct agent vices (e.g., reward-hacking, shortcut-taking, cheating) worth a dedicated search?

Is there ANY source documenting 'not-updating-beliefs' (an agent failing to revise its hypothesis in light of disconfirming evidence) in a research/science agent specifically? This WAGER vice is currently unrepresented in the confirmed set and should be actively hunted before concluding it is undocumented.

refuted.claim: On DiscoveryBench (autonomous data-driven scientific discovery), even the best LLM-agent system scores only 25%, so agents fail roughly three-quarters of discovery tasks — a direct measure of weak hypothesis-generation/discovery capability [vice: weak-hypothesis-or-taste].

There remains a 34.74% Relative Performance Gap (RPG) between the best AI agent and human data-science experts on realistic analysis tasks, quantifying how far these agents are from expert-level judgment (vice: weak-hypothesis-or-taste).
